text: This is Dr. Craig Rogers, Director of Renal Surgery at the Vatakuta Urology Institute at Henry Ford Hospital. In this video, we show kidney mobilization and exposing the tumor for optimal resection.
The surgeon uses the fourth robotic arm to adjust the position of the kidney, enhancing exposure of the tumor for resection.
The surgeon incises the Girotis fascia near the tumor, avoiding direct incision over the tumor itself.
The surgeon begins the exposure by incising over the normal kidney capsule, carefully working inward to reveal the tumor without breaching its surface.
The surgeon mobilizes the kidney to achieve a direct view of the tumor, adjusting its position for optimal exposure during the nephrectomy procedure.

The surgeon achieves ideal tumor exposure by positioning the kidney to provide a head-on view of the tumor, ensuring at least two centimeters of normal surrounding capsule are visible to allow for an adequate resection margin and placement of capsular sutures.
The surgeon repeats the exposure steps for the lower pole tumor, ensuring a clear view of the tumor with surrounding normal capsule tissue for adequate resection margin.
The surgeon uses the prograsp and the fourth robotic arm to reposition the kidney, enhancing the exposure of the tumor for precise surgical access.
In the next video, we demonstrate the use of intraoperative ultrasound to help define the tumor for resection.
